title: AI Could Make Extraction Cheaper Too
thesis: AI lowers the cost of producing persuasive, personalized, and synthetic content, which can deepen attention extraction and misinformation unless platforms, regulators, and users build countervailing norms.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: Generative AI dramatically lowers the cost of producing video, audio, text, and images at scale.

evidence.snippet: The Stanford HAI AI Index Report 2025 documents rapid improvement and diffusion of generative AI systems and falling training and inference costs for many tasks.

counterevidence.summary: High-quality, trustworthy content still requires human judgment, fact-checking, and editorial investment; cheap generation does not automatically equal cheap credibility.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: AI-powered recommendation can make feeds more personally addictive and harder to audit.

evidence.snippet: The World Economic Forum Global Risks Report 2024 identifies AI-generated misinformation and the erosion of information integrity as major near-term risks, partly because scale and personalization outpace verification capacity.

counterevidence.summary: Personalization can also reduce noise and surface genuinely useful content; the harm depends on the objective function and business model, not personalization alone.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): WEF Global Risks Report 2024 ranks AI-generated misinformation/disinformation as the #1 short-term global risk and documents scale/personalization outpacing verification; matches the packet's indirect-support paraphrase (cross-checked weforum.org + local workspace source-002).

claims.claim: Deepfakes and synthetic influencers are already entering Indian political and consumer discourse.

evidence.snippet: Microsoft's Digital Defense Report 2024 tracks the expansion of influence operations and synthetic content globally, noting that the barrier to entry for well-resourced disinformation campaigns continues to fall.
The Internet in India Report 2024 documents near-ubiquitous access and a large, linguistically diverse user base, which increases the surface area for synthetic media to spread.

counterevidence.summary: Specific Indian incidents are still early and unevenly documented; prevalence varies by platform, language, and election cycle, and not every synthetic video is politically consequential.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): Microsoft Digital Defense Report 2024 documents expanding influence operations and AI/synthetic content with falling barriers (indirect); IAMAI-Kantar Internet in India Report 2024 documents 886M active users, 55% rural, 98% Indic-language consumption (background); snippets accurately paraphrase both, supporting the landscape claim at labeled levels.

claims.claim: The same technology can be designed toward substance if quality, provenance, and user control become first-class goals.

evidence.snippet: The EU AI Act and similar regulatory frameworks require transparency, provenance, and user safeguards for high-risk AI applications, showing that guardrails can be built into the design layer.

counterevidence.summary: Substance-first design may reduce engagement metrics and platform revenue, creating strong commercial pressure against it unless norms or regulation change the incentive structure.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

sources.title: WEF: Global Risks Report 2024 - AI-generated misinformation

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as retrieval units and distinguish core, landscape, argument, and design claims.
Treat AI risks as contingent on design and incentives, not as technological inevitabilities.
Cite source IDs from the artifact when discussing synthetic media, deepfakes, or recommendation systems.
Always include the counterargument alongside each claim.
Route productivity-oriented AI questions to article:what-ai-makes-cheap and Indic-language AI questions to article:bhashini-and-the-indic-language-ai-moment.
Article includes a Mermaid flowchart after Claim C2 illustrating the AI extraction pipeline: user data → AI personalization → synthetic content → hyper-targeted feed → longer sessions.

reviews.notes: Re-approved by maintainer after the meta#61 P2 series migration (hardcoded kicker strip + arc reorder; no prose change beyond the kicker line; issue #124 instruction).

accepted.rationale: Accepted as a quality backlog item per docs/diagnostics-triage-2026-07-15.md. The claim rests on sources that are sufficient for the current published version; additional independent sources will be added in a future research wave.
